moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with this unusual first move, which defines the Van Geet Opening and immediately challenges Black's center. By placing our knight on c3 before establishing a pawn center, we keep options open while controlling the d5 and e4 squares—notice how this move prepares to support central play without committing to e4 yet. This flexible approach can catch unprepared opponents off guard, though it requires accurate follow-up.

Black naturally stakes a claim in the center with the e5 pawn, following classical opening principles. This move controls d4 and f4, giving Black space and preventing us from easily establishing a pawn on e4. Black is playing soundly here, not taking our opening too seriously but developing logically with good central presence.

We support the center with e3 rather than playing d4 immediately, which allows us to prepare the Hulsemann Gambit—a sharp tactical idea. By playing e3, we keep d4 available for a pawn break and prepare Qh5, which is the critical threat in this line. Notice we're being methodical about centralizing control before launching our attack.

Black expands in the center with d5, creating tension and fighting for space—this is a natural attempt to maintain equality. Black now controls both e4 and c4, and the position is becoming more concrete. However, this move also opens lines that we can exploit with our next move.

Here's the point of our setup: we unleash the Hulsemann Gambit with this attacking move, threatening both e5 and creating pressure along the h5-e2 diagonal. The queen on h5 is aggressively placed, forcing Black to defend carefully and preventing easy development. This move illustrates how the Van Geet can lead to sharp tactical play if White navigates accurately.

Black develops with purpose, placing the bishop on e6 where it defends against some of our attacking ideas while preparing to develop the kingside. The move also supports the d5 pawn and prepares castling, showing Black's practical approach to defending against our gambit attempt. Black is not overly worried yet but taking our threats seriously enough to organize the position.
